writing.task1: Here’s what happened: At 13:10 UTC we got notice of a prop redesign. By 15:00 UTC the fly-load check showed a 6% imbalance. At 15:20 UTC the call was made to delay re-calibration, and by 19:45 UTC we had an unscheduled hold. We rescheduled the audience, asked for one extra hire day ($X), and called supplier SLA. This raised chain-of-custody and insurance questions. Going forward: we need a mandatory day re-check and a clear re-calibration window before any evening cue-to-cue. Cost and reputation both at risk
writing.task2: I agree that all staged intimate work should require a certified intimacy coordinator and clear consent records. One case study showed productions with coordinators cut reported incidents by 60% while only adding about 3% rehearsal time. Some argue cost and artistic control, but safety and legal protection outweigh this. Small houses can use short accreditation and call-and-sign forms. Budget lines should cover this, as reduced risk and stronger actor trust save more than the small extra spend
writing.task3: Policy L saves more money (12%) but has higher replan risk (26%) and about 1.6 extra hire days, which can hurt reputation. Policy B saves less (4%) but risk is lower (6%) and only 0.2 extra days. For a company with tight budget but medium risk tolerance, Policy B is safer overall, as reputation loss is harder to fix than small savings. A good KPI is “weekly replan probability with 90% CI.” In Monte Carlo terms, median shows the most likely outcome, while CI shows the safe range of possible results

testData.testTitle: IELTS 4.1 — Advanced Acting & Production Operations — Practice Test (EN–VI)
